---
title: Dash
lightbox: true
---

[Dash](https://dash.plotly.com/) is ideal for building production-ready analytical applications. This guide shows you how to integrate querychat into your Dash applications.

![](/images/dash-app.png){fig-alt="Screenshot of querychat running in a Dash app." class="lightbox shadow rounded mb-3"}

## Installation

Install querychat with Dash support:
```bash
pip install "querychat[dash]"
```

## Quick start

The simplest way to get started is with `.app()`, which creates a complete Dash application:

```{.python filename="dash-app.py"}
from querychat.dash import QueryChat
from querychat.data import titanic

qc = QueryChat(titanic(), "titanic")
app = qc.app()

if __name__ == "__main__":
    app.run(debug=True)
```

Run with:
```bash
python dash-app.py
```

## State management

Dash uses callbacks and `dcc.Store` for state management. querychat provides:

- **`.store_id`** - The ID of the `dcc.Store` component holding query state
- **`.ui()`** - Returns the chat UI component
- **`.init_app(app)`** - Registers callbacks on your Dash app

The state dictionary (accessed via `Input(qc.store_id, "data")`) contains:

| Key | Type | Description |
|-----|------|-------------|
| `sql` | str \| None | Current SQL query |
| `title` | str \| None | Short description of current filter |
| `error` | str \| None | Error message if query failed |
| `chat_history` | list | Conversation history |

::: {.callout-tip}
Use the convenience methods `.df(state)`, `.sql(state)`, and `.title(state)` to access state values cleanly in your callbacks.
:::

## Custom layouts

For custom layouts, use `.ui()` and `.init_app()`:

```{.python filename="dash-custom.py"}
import dash_bootstrap_components as dbc
from dash import Dash, Input, Output, dcc, html
from dash.dash_table import DataTable

from querychat.dash import QueryChat
from querychat.data import titanic
from querychat.types import AppStateDict

qc = QueryChat(titanic(), "titanic")

app = Dash(__name__, external_stylesheets=[dbc.themes.BOOTSTRAP])

app.layout = dbc.Container([
    dbc.Row([
        # Left column: Chat
        dbc.Col(qc.ui(), width=4),

        # Right column: Data display
        dbc.Col([
            html.H3(id="data-title"),
            DataTable(id="data-table", page_size=10),
            html.Pre(id="sql-display"),
        ], width=8),
    ])
], fluid=True)

# Register querychat's internal callbacks
qc.init_app(app)

# Add your own callbacks using qc.store_id
@app.callback(
    [Output("data-title", "children"),
     Output("data-table", "data"),
     Output("data-table", "columns"),
     Output("sql-display", "children")],
    Input(qc.store_id, "data")
)
def update_display(state: AppStateDict):
    df = qc.df(state).to_pandas()
    sql = qc.sql(state) or f"SELECT * FROM {qc.data_source.table_name}"
    title = qc.title(state) or "All Data"

    columns = [{"name": c, "id": c} for c in df.columns]
    return title, df.to_dict("records"), columns, sql

if __name__ == "__main__":
    app.run(debug=True)
```

![](/images/dash-custom-app.png){fig-alt="Screenshot of custom Dash layout." class="lightbox shadow rounded mb-3"}

## Data views

Add Plotly visualizations that respond to query changes:

```{.python filename="dash-viz.py"}
import dash_bootstrap_components as dbc
import plotly.express as px
from dash import Dash, Input, Output, dcc, html

from querychat.dash import QueryChat
from querychat.data import titanic
from querychat.types import AppStateDict

qc = QueryChat(titanic(), "titanic")

app = Dash(__name__, external_stylesheets=[dbc.themes.BOOTSTRAP])

app.layout = dbc.Container([
    dbc.Row([
        dbc.Col(qc.ui(), width=4),
        dbc.Col([
            dcc.Graph(id="age-histogram"),
            dcc.Graph(id="class-survival"),
        ], width=8),
    ])
], fluid=True)

qc.init_app(app)

@app.callback(
    [Output("age-histogram", "figure"),
     Output("class-survival", "figure")],
    Input(qc.store_id, "data")
)
def update_charts(state: AppStateDict):
    df = qc.df(state).to_pandas()

    fig1 = px.histogram(df, x="age", color="survived",
                        title="Age Distribution")
    fig2 = px.bar(df.groupby("pclass")["survived"].mean().reset_index(),
                  x="pclass", y="survived", title="Survival by Class")

    return fig1, fig2

if __name__ == "__main__":
    app.run(debug=True)
```

![](/images/dash-viz.png){fig-alt="Screenshot of Dash data views example." class="lightbox shadow rounded mb-3"}

## Reset functionality

Add a reset button using Dash callbacks:

```{.python filename="dash-reset.py"}
import dash_bootstrap_components as dbc
from dash import Dash, Input, Output, State, callback_context, html
from dash.dash_table import DataTable

from querychat.dash import QueryChat
from querychat.data import titanic
from querychat.types import AppStateDict

qc = QueryChat(titanic(), "titanic")

app = Dash(__name__, external_stylesheets=[dbc.themes.BOOTSTRAP])

app.layout = dbc.Container([
    dbc.Row([
        dbc.Col([
            qc.ui(),
            dbc.Button("Reset Filters", id="reset-btn", class_name="mt-2 w-100"),
        ], width=4),
        dbc.Col([
            DataTable(id="data-table", page_size=10),
        ], width=8),
    ])
], fluid=True)

qc.init_app(app)

@app.callback(
    [Output("data-table", "data"),
     Output("data-table", "columns")],
    [Input(qc.store_id, "data"),
     Input("reset-btn", "n_clicks")],
)
def update_table(state: AppStateDict, reset_clicks):
    ctx = callback_context
    triggered = ctx.triggered[0]["prop_id"] if ctx.triggered else ""

    if "reset-btn" in triggered:
        df = qc.data_source.get_data().to_pandas()
    else:
        df = qc.df(state).to_pandas()

    columns = [{"name": c, "id": c} for c in df.columns]
    return df.to_dict("records"), columns

if __name__ == "__main__":
    app.run(debug=True)
```

![](/images/dash-reset.png){fig-alt="Screenshot of Dash reset functionality example." class="lightbox shadow rounded mb-3"}

## Complete example

Here's a full example with metrics, data table, and visualizations:

```{.python filename="dash-complete.py"}
import dash_bootstrap_components as dbc
import plotly.express as px
from dash import Dash, Input, Output, dcc, html
from dash.dash_table import DataTable

from querychat.dash import QueryChat
from querychat.data import titanic
from querychat.types import AppStateDict

qc = QueryChat(titanic(), "titanic")

app = Dash(__name__, external_stylesheets=[dbc.themes.BOOTSTRAP])

app.layout = dbc.Container([
    html.H1("Titanic Dataset Explorer", className="my-3"),

    dbc.Row([
        # Left: Chat
        dbc.Col(qc.ui(), width=4),

        # Right: Dashboard
        dbc.Col([
            # Title
            html.H3(id="data-title", className="mb-3"),

            # Metrics
            dbc.Row([
                dbc.Col(dbc.Card([
                    dbc.CardHeader("Passengers"),
                    dbc.CardBody(html.H4(id="metric-passengers")),
                ])),
                dbc.Col(dbc.Card([
                    dbc.CardHeader("Survivors"),
                    dbc.CardBody(html.H4(id="metric-survivors")),
                ])),
                dbc.Col(dbc.Card([
                    dbc.CardHeader("Survival Rate"),
                    dbc.CardBody(html.H4(id="metric-rate")),
                ])),
            ], className="mb-3"),

            # Tabs
            dbc.Tabs([
                dbc.Tab([
                    DataTable(
                        id="data-table",
                        page_size=10,
                        style_table={"overflowX": "auto"},
                    )
                ], label="Data"),
                dbc.Tab([
                    dcc.Graph(id="age-chart"),
                ], label="Charts"),
                dbc.Tab([
                    html.Pre(id="sql-display", className="bg-light p-3"),
                ], label="SQL"),
            ]),
        ], width=8),
    ])
], fluid=True)

qc.init_app(app)

@app.callback(
    [Output("data-title", "children"),
     Output("metric-passengers", "children"),
     Output("metric-survivors", "children"),
     Output("metric-rate", "children"),
     Output("data-table", "data"),
     Output("data-table", "columns"),
     Output("age-chart", "figure"),
     Output("sql-display", "children")],
    Input(qc.store_id, "data")
)
def update_all(state: AppStateDict):
    df = qc.df(state).to_pandas()
    sql = qc.sql(state) or f"SELECT * FROM {qc.data_source.table_name}"
    title = qc.title(state) or "All Data"

    # Metrics
    n_passengers = len(df)
    n_survivors = int(df["survived"].sum())
    survival_rate = f"{df['survived'].mean():.1%}"

    # Table
    columns = [{"name": c, "id": c} for c in df.columns]

    # Chart
    fig = px.histogram(df, x="age", color="survived",
                       title="Age Distribution by Survival")

    return (
        title,
        n_passengers, n_survivors, survival_rate,
        df.to_dict("records"), columns,
        fig, sql
    )

if __name__ == "__main__":
    app.run(debug=True)
```

![](/images/dash-complete.png){fig-alt="Screenshot of complete Dash app with querychat." class="lightbox shadow rounded mb-3"}

## Deployment

Dash apps can be deployed to various platforms:

- **[Posit Connect](https://posit.co/products/enterprise/connect/)** - Enterprise deployment
- **[Heroku](https://devcenter.heroku.com/articles/getting-started-with-python)** - Cloud platform
- **[AWS/GCP/Azure](https://dash.plotly.com/deployment)** - Cloud providers

Remember to set your API keys as environment variables in your deployment environment.

## See also

- [Greet users](greet.qmd) - Create welcoming onboarding experiences
- [Provide context](context.qmd) - Help the LLM understand your data better
